name: evaluating student engagement platforms for scalability
top_issues: No grade-band specificity anywhere in the article — 'students,' 'classrooms,' and 'diverse learners' are used throughout with zero examples tied to actual grade levels (e.g., 'elementary rostering challenges differ from high school elective scheduling'). A K-5 curriculum director and a high school principal have completely different scalability concerns, and this article doesn't acknowledge that.

Wayground's value proposition is generic and tacked on at the very end: 'Wayground offers an inclusive, standards-aligned supplemental learning platform with automatic accommodations and AI-supported differentiation' tells a district leader nothing actionable. What specific feature handles tiered content? How does it connect to Clever? This reads like a boilerplate ad tag, not a demonstrated capability.

The article is written almost entirely for district technology leaders and curriculum coordinators — not classroom teachers. The framing ('evaluate vendors,' 'signed DPAs,' '99.9% uptime SLAs') will be opaque to the building-level teacher the blog likely intends to reach, and the teacher agency framing is inconsistent: phrases like 'when platforms handle the technical complexity of differentiation, teachers can focus on what they do best' subtly position the platform as doing the instructional heavy lifting.
positive_signals: Standards and research citations are genuinely strong — ISTE, CoSN, FERPA, CAST, WCAG 2.1, RAND, and the Uptime Institute are all named and connected to specific claims, which gives district leaders real frameworks to act on.

The staged pilot structure (3 schools → benchmarks → 56+ classrooms) with concrete go/no-go thresholds (80% adoption, sub-2-second load times, one class period of prep savings) gives procurement teams a replicable process rather than vague advice.
narrative: This article is well-researched and structurally sound for a district technology audience, but it falls short of a classroom-teacher pass on two criteria that trigger mandatory revision: grade-band specificity is entirely absent (a K-2 teacher and a high school department chair face fundamentally different scalability realities, and the article treats them as interchangeable), and Wayground's mention at the end is a single generic sentence that fails to demonstrate any specific feature or use case. The audience mismatch is also a problem — the article speaks fluently to CTOs and curriculum coordinators but would lose most building-level teachers at "signed Data Processing Agreements" and "EVER criteria," with no on-ramp provided. To pass, the author should anchor at least two concrete grade-band examples throughout (e.g., "for middle school ELA departments managing intervention periods…"), replace the Wayground tag with one specific feature and a 30-second use case, and define or briefly gloss the procurement-layer terminology for teacher readers.
